topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery smelled faintly of turpentine and old varnish, a familiar comfort that usually settled my nerves. I was meticulously cleaning brushes, the silence of the studio amplifying the tiny sounds of my movements. Then I saw it. Tucked beneath the edge of my latest charcoal sketch, a faint, almost invisible line of ink. It was a precise, intricate pattern, clearly a tattoo, and it was unmistakably a rendering of my most recent abstract piece. My hand froze mid-wipe. A coldness bloomed in my chest, tightening my throat until I had to consciously inhale. I slowly leaned closer, my breath catching in my lungs. The detail was unnervingly perfect, the shading flawless. Who had done this? The thought sent a tremor through my fingers, making the bristles of the brush tremble. I backed away, bumping against a stack of canvases, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet room.

[story 2]

It happened during a casual visit to the local art supply shop. I was browsing for specific pigments when I noticed a piece of fabric draped over a display stand—a scarf, perhaps. As I reached out to touch it, I saw it. Etched onto the silk, in delicate, dark lines, was a miniature version of the composition I had been struggling with for weeks. My heart gave a sudden, hard thump against my ribs. I pulled my hand back as if burned, my knuckles white. I stared at the scarf, my vision blurring slightly around the edges. It felt like being watched, a prickling sensation crawling up my neck. I quickly moved toward the exit, needing the open air, the noise of the street to drown out the strange, heavy feeling settling in my stomach.

[story 3]

I was reviewing my portfolio late one evening, the harsh glare of the desk lamp illuminating the canvas. I traced the outline of a particularly complex figure I’d been working on. That’s when I noticed the anomaly. Embedded within the shadows of the figure, a subtle, almost hidden design caught my eye—a series of sharp, stylized lines that mirrored the very structure of my composition. I leaned in, squinting, my focus sharpening to an almost painful degree. It wasn't a mistake; it was deliberate. A slow, creeping awareness began to spread through me, heavy and suffocating. I snatched my hand away from the canvas, my gaze darting around the room as if searching for an unseen presence. Every shadow seemed deeper, every quiet creak of the building sounded like a deliberate step.
